chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Foundations of Software Engineering. (3-2). Credit 4. Intensive programming experience and provision of the fundamentals needed for larger-scale software development; integration of concepts in computer science and familiarization with a variety of programming and development tools and techniques; team projects each with an emphasis on a different specialization within computer science; emphasis on programming techniques to ease code integration and clarity; practical exposure to software-engineering processes through large-scale projects and specification and documentation.

chunks.title: Course Prerequisites
chunks.content:
Prerequisite/Corequisite(s): Grade of C or better in CSCE 314, CSCE 350/ECEN 350, or ECEN 350/CSCE 350; grade of C or better or concurrent enrollment in CSCE 313.
For students taking CSCE 313 concurrently: If you decide to Q-drop CSCE 313 to defer it to a future semester, you will not be able to remain in this course. Both courses are needed to satisfy the curriculum's overall design and to prepare you for later CSCE technical courses.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
• Explain the need for software engineering through analysis of industry examples and personal experience.
• Apply fundamental concepts of software construction, including requirements management, design, implementation, testing, and deployment.
• Design and develop software that is clear, maintainable, and integrates current software technologies, including new and unfamiliar tools.
• Collaborate and communicate effectively in small teams to achieve project goals.
• Produce technical documentation using characteristics of effective technical writing.
• Analyze software system requirements for portability, performance, and testability.
• Employ coding layout and style conventions to enhance code clarity and ease of integration.
• Utilize software tools and APIs to augment software functionality and maintainability.
• Implement database solutions and design user interfaces that meet project specifications.

chunks.title: Special Course Designation
chunks.content:
UWRT - University Required Writing Intensive
All Texas A&M University undergraduates must pass two courses in their major that fulfill the graduation requirement for writing and oral communication. These W/C courses support you in becoming a proficient writer and speaker in our discipline.
This course is a writing-intensive (W) course. You will practice the types of writing you will encounter in the workplace or academic careers in our discipline. As a writing-intensive course, you must pass the writing components with a C or better to pass the course.
This course provides writing instruction and multiple opportunities to develop and practice your skills. You will also receive formative feedback from which you can revise your writing.
In addition, the University Writing Center (UWC) provides both writing and speaking.guides for additional self-study and has trained peer consultants available to support you on your work.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
Provided electronically at no cost through Perusall and linked on Canvas.
Notes:
Each and every Perusall reading assignment must be accessed from Canvas to receive credit and for grades to transfer to Canvas.

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content: Your grade for this course reflects your mastery of course material and is determined by multiple components. As a writing course, you must pass the writing components with a C or better to earn a passing grade in the course. Failure to pass the writing components with a C or better results in an F for the course.

chunks.title: Grading Components
chunks.content:
Specific grading practices will be announced when assignments are given through assignment descriptions and assignment rubrics. When a rubric is given, the work must meet the condition of Minimum Viable Product (MVP)-quality before being assessed by the rubric.
• MVP-quality is defined in this context as the work must be at least non-trivially complete, of appropriate quality, and demonstrative of genuine effort.
Each graded assignment in this course falls under one of the following assignment categories:
• Project Elements
• Project 1: Website Development: An individual project on website development.
• Project 2: Databases and Waterfall: A team Waterfall project on databases.
• Project 3: Full Stack Web Application Development: A team Agile project on web application development.
• Written Elements: This is a writing intensive course. You are required to pass this component with a C or better to earn a grade in the course. You will have several written assignments within the projects
• Class Elements
• Readings: Materials such as book chapters, articles, and videos that present course topics.
• Recall Practices: Quizzes that check your knowledge of the readings.
• In-Class Activities: Group activities that apply your knowledge of the readings.
• Workday Reports: Reports that overview completed workday productivity.
• Lab Activities: Individual and team activities that demonstrate evidence of lab objectives.
• Assessment Elements
• Peer Evaluations: For the team projects, you will have multiple opportunities to submit peer evaluations and corresponding reflections that assess your teammates and yourself, respectively.
• Individual Performances: Your individual performance to the success of the team projects and group assignments will be graded. In any team environment, striving to be a valuable member of your team is critical. Assessment sources such as logged code commits, peer evaluations, attendance, participation, retrospectives, confidential remarks to the instructor, and instructor/TA observations will be evaluated for this grade.

chunks.title: Grading Calculation
chunks.content:
Works will be calculated with the specifications grading approach. Each work will be evaluated to determine if it meets the specifications. Calculations will be based on the specification grading's EMRN scale:
• [S+] Highly Satisfactory (i.e., Excellent/Exemplary): The work meets or exceeds the expectations of the assignment. Communication is clear and complete. Mastery of concepts is evidence. There are no nontrivial errors. This work could be used as a classroom example.
• [S] Satisfactory (i.e., Meets Expectations): Understanding of the concepts is evident through correct work and clear, audience-appropriate explanations. Some revision or expansion is needed, but no significant gaps or errors are present. No additional instruction on the concepts is needed.
• [U] Unsatisfactory (i.e., Revision Needed): Partial understanding of the concepts is evident, but there are significant gaps that remain. Needs further work, more review, and/or improved explanations.
• [U-] Highly Unsatisfactory (i.e., Not Assessable): Not enough information is present in the work to determine whether there is understanding of the concepts. The work is fragmentary or contains significant omissions. Or there are too many issues to justify correcting each one.
For assignments hosted on their respective platforms (e.g., Canvas, Perusall, Gradescope), the EMRN scale is mapped numerically as follows:
• [S+]: Highly Satisfactory as 1½ or more points.
• [S]: Satisfactory as 1 point.
• [U]: Unsatisfactory as ½ point.
• [U-]: Highly Unsatisfactory as 0 points.

chunks.title: Grading Scale
chunks.content:
Works will be assigned scores that fall under one of two level:
• [S*] Satisfactory Type: a satisfactory score [S] or a highly satisfactory score [S+]
• [U*] Unsatisfactory Type: an unsatisfactory score [U] or a highly unsatisfactory score [U-]
The following table shows the maximum allowable number of [U*] scores for each assignment category per letter grade. Note that assignment category letter grades are therefore calculated by the number of [U*] scores—not by the average of assignment scores—in that category.
Grading scale of maximum allowable number of U* scores per letter grade for each assignment category.
| Assignment Element | Assignment Category | A | B | C | D | F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Project Elements | Project 1 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| Project Elements | Project 2 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| Project Elements | Project 3 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| Written Elements | Written Elements | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| Class Elements | Readings | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| Class Elements | Recall Practices | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| Class Elements | In-Class Activities | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| Class Elements | Workday Reports | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| Class Elements | Lab Activities | 6 | 7 | 8 | 9 | 10 |
| Assessment Elements | Peer Evaluations | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
| Assessment Elements | Individual Performances | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
Your overall course letter grade will be based on the lowest letter grade of all the assignment categories. These letter grades represent varying degrees of achievement (see the university's grading system):
• A = excellent, B = good, C = satisfactory, D = passing, F = failing

chunks.title: Submission of Assignments
chunks.content: All works must be submitted electronically—either directly through Canvas or indirectly through Canvas-connected external tools (e.g., Gradescope, Persuall, Peerceptiv).

chunks.title: Resubmission of Assignments
chunks.content:
Only the following assignment categories are eligible for resubmission of work that was initially scored as [U]:
• Project Elements: Project 1, Project 2, Project 3
• Written Elements: all
The conditions for resubmission are as follows:
• previous submission is MVP-quality
• resubmission fully addresses the work's feedback
• resubmission includes an explanatory remark in the assignment's comment section of what specifically was unsatisfactory and how it was addressed
• resubmission occurs up to one week after the initial deadline
• only one resubmission is guaranteed to be allowed per assignment by default
• any additional resubmission is regraded at the discretion of the instructor

chunks.title: Redemption of Assignments
chunks.content:
Assignment redemptions are opportunities for works with [U*] scores to be redeemed as [S] by other works with [S*] scores in the same assignment category.
Only the following assignment categories are eligible for redemption of work that was initially scored as [U*]:
• Class Elements: Recall Practices
• Each work with a [U*] score can be redeemed with two works with [S+] scores.
• Assessment Elements: Individual Performances
• Each work with a [U] score can be redeemed from one subsequent work with an [S+] score.
• Each work with a [U-] score can be redeemed from two subsequent works with [S+] scores.

chunks.title: Late Work Policy
chunks.content: Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy (Student Rule 7).

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content:
Work submitted from assignments in the following eligible categories are accepted as late work without penalty up to two days after their posted deadline, under the condition that the work is of Minimally Viable Product (MVP)-quality:
• Project Elements: Project 1, Project 2, Project 3
• Written Elements: all
• Class Elements: Readings
The added condition for the late work to be accepted is that it is of Minimally Viable Product (MVP)-quality as defined in the Grading Policy section.
Any work that is either not from assignments of eligible categories or is below MVP-quality will not be accepted as late work.

chunks.title: Accessibility Statement
chunks.content:
Howdy! Your CSCE 489 instructors want you to succeed in this challenging course. While the Disability Accommodation Portal (AIM Portal) makes it easier to share accommodation information, we ask that you also schedule a meeting with your section's instructor to discuss your accommodations in person. In addition to accommodations, we're happy to talk about strategies for succeeding in this course.
We strongly encourage you to secure your accommodations—even if you're unsure you'll need them. When Dr. Moore began teaching at Texas A&M, he noticed a concerning pattern: students often chose to take the first exam without using their approved accommodations. Many said they wanted to "try it without them." After the first exam, there was often a rush of accommodation requests. These instances have decreased significantly since we've been more explicit about our preferences.
While Dr. Moore suspects that some students hesitate due to negative experiences with previous instructors, we want you to know that our department culture is one of support. We are committed to providing accommodations approved by Disability Resources with professionalism and care.
"We prefer that you get your accommodations and not need them, rather than trying without your accommodations and finding out that you needed them."
Finally, everyone should be mindful of creating software and digital artifacts that are accessible. If you're majoring in computer science or computer engineering, consider taking CSCE 432: Accessible Computing, a course developed by Dr. Moore to help students design with accessibility in mind.

chunks.title: Professionalism and Respect
chunks.content:
You are expected to treat your instructor and all other participants in the course with courtesy and respect. Your comments to others should be factual, constructive, and free from harassing statements. You are encouraged to disagree with other students, but such disagreements need to be based upon facts and documentation (rather than prejudices and personalities).
Professional and respectful engagement is expected at all times; failure to do so may impact assignment grades.
Students need to contribute in intelligent, positive, and constructive manners within the course. Behaviors that are abusive, disruptive, or harassing may result in disciplinary actions as specified within the Student Rules.
Sensitive or controversial topics might be discussed in this course. Comments posted within this course are to remain confidential to the audience of this course; do not copy or share messages or writings from this course with others not in this course section.
Students in this course are also responsible for being familiar with the University student rules and policies.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools—such as ChatGPT, Copilot, Claude, and others—are rapidly changing the practice of software engineering. In this course, you will be both required and encouraged to use AI in structured, intentional ways to deepen your understanding of how these tools affect your workflow and outputs. The course policy below is intended to help you learn how to use modern AI tools responsibly and ethically in software engineering.

chunks.title: Required Uses of AI
chunks.content: Certain assignments will direct you to use AI tools for specific tasks (e.g., generating code comments, comparing your code to AI-generated code, or improving writing). Follow all instructions for these assignments precisely, including any prompt engineering or documentation requirements.

chunks.title: Permitted Uses of AI
chunks.content:
AI may be used to assist with limited, well-defined tasks (such as debugging or suggesting code comments), but may not be used to design your entire project or to complete assignments whose primary learning outcomes involve your own design and implementation.
Direct use of AI to generate complete solutions for programming assignments, project designs, or other major tasks is prohibited unless explicitly stated otherwise in the assignment instructions.

chunks.title: AI and Academic Integrity Requirements
chunks.content:
For any usage of AI in any part of your work—including explicit assignment directions—refer to the following points in order to maintain academic integrity:
• Clearly cite any AI-generated content by listing each tool, document prompts, and output, as well as explaining how the content was created.
• Properly attribute AI-generated material, since failure to do so is considered academic misconduct and will be reported to the Aggie Honor System Office.
• Limit usage of no more than 30% of any assignment to AI-generated content.

chunks.title: Learning Resources
chunks.content:
In addition to office hours with the instructor and TAs, you have access to many academic supports as a student. These supports are intended for you to achieve the academic success you are truly capable of. Most students access academic support at some point while pursuing their degree. Here are a few that are most relevant to this course.
Peer Teacher Central
Peer Teacher Central (PTC) provides a group of students you can talk to for help in 100, 200, and 300 level CSCE courses. Sometimes it is more comfortable talking to a fellow student instead of the instructor or TA. You can get help from students who have successfully completed this course in person on the first floor of the Peterson building in Room 127 or after hours on their discord server. You will also have access to peer teachers in the lab. Students consistently rate peer teachers as having a positive impact on their academic performance.
University Writing Center
Writing and speaking are challenging. The University Writing Center has trained peer consultants available to work with you on any kind of writing or speaking project, including research papers, lab reports, application essays, or creative writing, and at any stage of your process, whether you're deciding on a topic or reviewing your final draft. You can also get help with public speaking, presentations, and group projects. You can schedule in-person and Zoom appointments, browse their writing and speaking.guides for additional self-study, or email uwc@tamu.edu for help.

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content:
Every lecture and every lab will consist of at least one active class assignment that is due during that lecture and lab, respectively.
• Lectures consist of recall practices and in-class activities for lecture activity days, and workday reports for class workdays.
• Labs consist of lab activities.
Satisfactory completion of each class assignment requires proper evidence of physical presence, genuine participation, and correct execution of that assignment.
Refer to the Grading Policy section for the number of allowable unsatisfactory assignment scores for lectures and labs.

chunks.title: Course Specific Makeup Work Policy
chunks.content:
If you must miss a class or lab for a legitimate and documented reason (see Student Rule 7), then submit valid absence documentation to the applicable assignment on Canvas by navigating to: Modules → Student Resources → Valid Absence Request Documentation.
• Note: Documentation will not be accepted by email.
